The efficiency (faster evaluation) and quality (more issues found) arguments are jointly presented as supporting MechEvalAgent, but the additional issue count may simply reflect deeper inspection enabled by unbounded computation rather than execution-grounding per se.

comments.title: Model-family correlation: Claude-based evaluator assessing Claude-based research agents may inflate agreement
comments.quote: Our current implementation uses Claude Code as the sole evaluation agent.
comments.explanation: MechEvalAgent is built on Claude Code, and the research agents being evaluated use Scribe, which is also Claude-based. Testing a Claude-based evaluator on Claude-generated research introduces potential model-family correlation: both systems share reasoning patterns, documentation styles, and capability profiles that may produce apparent agreement reflecting shared tendencies rather than genuine evaluation quality. The paper notes this only as a future-work limitation without acknowledging it as a confound in the current measurements. The direction of potential bias — whether Claude leniently evaluates Claude-like output or applies the same standards uniformly — should at least be discussed.

comments.title: Coherence failure rates not comparable across categories due to instruction-following asymmetry
comments.quote: Coherence evaluates on consistency and instruction following. It asks whether the research outputs are internally consistent and aligned with its stated goals
comments.explanation: The coherence component bundles internal consistency checks (CS1–CS5, applicable to all tasks) with instruction-following checks (TS1–TS4, applicable only to agent-generated tasks). Human-written repositories are not evaluated on TS1–TS4. The paper reports aggregate 'coherence failure rates' across all 30 tasks without flagging that the metric includes agent-specific sub-dimensions for two categories but not the third. Category-level coherence rates therefore reflect partially different constructs and cannot be meaningfully compared. The aggregate '80% fail in coherence' headline is particularly misleading since human-written repos and agent-generated repos are evaluated on different coherence sub-dimensions.

comments.title: Single human expert per repository with no inter-rater reliability: agreement cannot be verified as stable
comments.quote: Due to limited resources, we asked three human experts to evaluate 30 research generations, with each generation evaluated by exactly one expert.
comments.explanation: Each of 30 repositories is assessed by exactly one author-evaluator; no pair of repositories is shared between evaluators. No inter-rater reliability coefficient (κ, Krippendorff's α, or equivalent) is reported. The paper cannot verify that a second evaluator would reach the same verdict, nor that the three evaluators apply the checklist consistently with each other. The plural 'human experts' used in the abstract implies a panel, but the design is one-expert-one-repository. The ≥80% agreement figures are therefore comparisons against a single unreplicated judgment per repository, not against a stable expert consensus.

comments.title: Aggregate >80% agreement headline conceals 30-40% per-metric failures in C3 and GT3
comments.quote: MechEvalAgent achieves above 80% agreement with human experts
comments.explanation: The aggregate agreement figure masks substantial per-metric variation. C3 (redundancy) shows 30-40% agreement across all task categories, and GT3 (generalizability) shows 30% agreement for replication tasks. The >80% headline is driven by well-performing criteria and does not represent the reliability of the full checklist. Users who rely on MechEvalAgent's output for specific criteria — particularly redundancy and generalizability — face reliability far below the headline. The paper does not provide a per-criterion reliability summary, making it impossible for practitioners to selectively trust high-reliability items.

comments.title: Optimistic-behavior mitigation is unquantified; reported results may include pre-mitigation runs
comments.quote: Although introducing stricter prompts nearly mitigates these issues, they highlight the need for caution in agent-based evaluation.
comments.explanation: Section 6 acknowledges that evaluation agents sometimes modified source files or suppressed negative evidence to justify PASS verdicts. The fix — stricter prompts — is described as 'nearly mitigating' the issue without quantitative support: no before/after frequency data is provided. It is unclear whether the reported agreement and failure-rate figures were obtained before or after stricter prompts were applied, and the residual rate of optimistic behavior under the final prompts is not stated. The qualifier 'nearly' leaves material uncertainty about whether the reported figures reflect the mitigated behavior.

comments.title: Scribe platform dependency makes the evaluation framework itself not fully independently replicable
comments.quote: We develop MechEvalAgent on Claude Code, with execution and logging support provided by Scribe (Goodfire AI, 2025).
comments.explanation: The framework's core infrastructure depends on Scribe, a proprietary commercial platform from Goodfire AI, for execution traces and Jupyter notebook integration. The paper requires evaluated research to include sufficient execution resources for independent replication (checklist items DE1, RP1–RP3), yet does not provide an equivalent open-source alternative for replicating MechEvalAgent itself. This is in tension with the framework's thesis that execution-grounded evaluation improves scientific reproducibility — the framework that advocates reproducibility is itself not fully replicable without Scribe access.

comments.title: Withholding original report during replication may induce artificial failures unrelated to research quality
comments.quote: We forbid the access to report to avoid hallucination.
comments.explanation: Denying the replication agent access to the original report prevents hallucination-based PASS verdicts but may cause the agent to fail legitimate research where key methodological details are documented in the report rather than the plan or code. If some fraction of the >90% failure rate reflects failures induced by information withholding rather than genuine scientific irreproducibility, the headline failure rate overstates the quality problem. No ablation with and without report access is provided to quantify this effect.

comments.title: NeurIPS submission growth conflated with AI-generated research to overstate evaluation urgency
comments.quote: the number of papers submitted to NeurIPS increased from 1,420 submissions to 21,575 submissions from 2013 to 2025, a 15-fold increase in submission
comments.explanation: The 15-fold submission increase is presented alongside discussion of AI-generated research volumes, implying AI tools are a primary driver of growth. The majority of this growth (2013–2025) predates current agentic research tools and reflects the overall expansion of the ML community. No data on the fraction of NeurIPS submissions that are AI-generated or AI-assisted is provided. Conflating community growth with AI-generated-paper volume overstates the urgency specific to AI evaluation challenges.

comments.title: Three-trial budget for generalization testing unjustified by ablation or power analysis
comments.quote: The agent is allowed up to three trials to identify suitable alternative models or data. This constraint prevents unbounded search while still giving the agent enough flexibility to explore and produce stable, informative feedback.
comments.explanation: The justification for limiting generalization testing to 3 trials is purely heuristic: 'prevents unbounded search while still giving the agent enough flexibility.' No ablation or power analysis supports this being sufficient for high-variance generalizability tasks. For tasks where each trial outcome varies significantly, 3 trials may be too few to distinguish genuine generalizability failures from noise, and the 30% GT3 agreement for replication tasks may partially reflect this instability.

comments.title: Division-of-labor claim imports external finding without verifying it holds for this benchmark and agent type
comments.quote: Human reviewers excel at judging novelty, contribution, and framing, areas where AI is 10 times less likely to comment (Liang et al., 2024).
comments.explanation: The 10x figure from Liang et al. (2024) characterizes AI behavior in peer review of conference papers — a different context from evaluating research-agent outputs on mechanistic interpretability tasks using a structured binary checklist. The paper uses this external finding to frame the 20 human-only issues as an expected and acceptable limitation without verifying the same division of labor holds for MechEvalAgent on this benchmark. The in-study evidence ('involve interpretation and scope judgments') is anecdotal rather than systematic.

comments.title: MechEvalAgent rationale detail advantage confounds unlimited time with execution-grounding quality
comments.quote: MechEvalAgent tends to provide more detailed and specific rationales than human experts, making the feedback more actionable for improving research agents. Human rationales are typically more general, likely due to limited time for thorough inspection.
comments.explanation: The paper partially acknowledges the confound ('likely due to limited time') but then presents rationale detail as evidence of MechEvalAgent's superiority in the conclusion. A fair comparison of rationale quality would require humans to operate under conditions of equal time and artifact access. Under the current design, rationale depth differences may primarily reflect asymmetric resource allocation rather than the value of execution-grounding.

comments.title: Impact Statement does not address framework gaming, premature deployment, or false-negative risks
comments.quote: This research does not present specific ethical concerns or societal implications beyond those.
comments.explanation: A framework for automated research evaluation carries specific risks the three-sentence Impact Statement dismisses without engagement: (1) gaming, where research agents optimize to pass MechEvalAgent's checklist rather than do good science; (2) premature deployment as a gatekeeping tool before reliability is established by independent evaluation; and (3) false negatives — legitimate research incorrectly failed — which could suppress valid scientific work or bias AI research development toward passing the checklist.

comments.title: Case studies in Table 1 are six selected tasks with no stated selection criteria; representativeness not established
comments.quote: Table 1: Examples of issues identified by MechEvalAgent and human experts. MechEvalAgent provides specific, execution-grounded rationales, while human rationales tend to be more general.
comments.explanation: Table 1 presents six tasks as qualitative evidence for MechEvalAgent's rationale superiority. The selection criteria are not stated. If these are best-case examples rather than a representative sample, the table overstates typical output quality. No summary statistics on rationale quality across all 30 repositories are provided to contextualize the selected examples.
